content:
Let $X(\omega)$ be the Fourier transform of the signal

$x(t) = e^{-t^4} \cos t, \quad -\infty < t < \infty$.

The value of the derivative of $X(\omega)$ at $\, \omega = 0$ is ______ (rounded off to 1 decimal place).
A function f(t) is shown in the figure. The Fourier transform F($$\mathrm\omega$$) of f(t) is

A signal is represented by $$$x\left(t\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}1\;\;\;\left|t\right|\;<\;1\\0\;\;\;\left|t\right|\;>\;1\end{array}\right.$$$
The Fourier transform of the convolved signal y(t)=x(2t) * x(t/2) is

An ideal low pass filter has frequency response given by

$$
H(j \omega)= \begin{cases}1, & |\omega| \leq 200 \pi \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases}
$$


Let $h(t)$ be its time domain representation. Then $h(0)=$ ____________ (round off to the nearest integer)
explanation:
$$ 1^{\text {st }} \text { Method : } $$

$$ H(j \omega)=\operatorname{rec}\left(\frac{\omega}{400 \pi}\right) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} h(t) & =\frac{\sin (200 \pi t)}{\pi t}=\frac{200 \sin (200 \pi t)}{200 \pi t} \\ \left.h(t)\right|_{t=0} & =200 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &2^{\text {nd }} \text { Method : }\\& &\begin{aligned} & h(0)=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} H(j \omega) d \omega \\ & h(0)=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{-200 \pi}^{200 \pi} 1 d \omega=\frac{1}{2 \pi}[400 \pi]=200 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content:
Let an input x(t) = 2 sin(10$$\pi$$t) + 5 cos(15$$\pi$$t) + 7 sin(42$$\pi$$t) + 4 cos(45$$\pi$$t) is passed through an LTI system having an impulse response,

$$h(t) = 2\left( {{{\sin (10\pi t)} \over {\pi t}}} \right)\cos (40\pi t)$$

The output of the system is
explanation:
Given that:

$$x(t) = 2\sin (10\pi t) + 5cos(15\pi t) + 7\sin (42\pi t) + 4\cos (45\pi t)$$

$$h(t) = 2\left[ {{{\sin (10\pi t)} \over {\pi t}}} \right]\cos (40\pi t)$$

$$h(t) = 2g(t)\cos (40\pi t)$$

Apply Fourier transform

$$H(\omega ) = 2\left[ {{{G(\omega - 40\pi ) + G(\omega + 40\pi )} \over 2}} \right] = G(\omega - 40\omega ) + G(\omega + 40\omega )$$

$$\mathop {2\sin (10\pi t)}\limits_{{\omega _1} = 10\pi } \, + \,\mathop {5\cos (15\pi t)}\limits_{{\omega _2} = 15\pi } \, + \mathop {7\sin (42\pi t)}\limits_{{\omega _3} = 42\pi } \, + \mathop {4\cos (45\pi t)}\limits_{{\omega _4} = 45\pi } $$

The output of LTI system is

$$y(t) = 7\sin (42\pi t) + 4\cos (45\pi t)$$

content:
Let $f(t)$ be an even function, i.e., $f(-t)=f(t)$ for all $t$. Let the Fourier transform of $f(t)$ be defined as

$F(\omega)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-j \omega t} d t$. Suppose $\frac{d F(\omega)}{d \omega}=-\omega F(\omega)$ for all $\omega$ and $F(0)=1$. Then
explanation:
Given :

(i) $f(t)$ is even i.e. $f(-t)=f(t)$

(ii) $\frac{d F(\omega)}{d \omega}=-\omega F(\omega)$ for all $\omega$

(iii) $F(0)=1$

The only even function, whose differentiation contains itself is a Gaussian function represented as $e^{-a \omega^2}$

Selecting a Gaussian function which satisfies all mentioned condition,

$$ \begin{aligned} & F(\omega)=e^{-\frac{\omega^2}{2}} \\ & \frac{d}{d \omega} F(\omega)=-\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \omega \times e^{-\frac{\omega^2}{2}}=-\omega F(\omega) \\ & F(0)=\left.e^{-\frac{\omega^2}{2}}\right|_{\omega=0}=1 \end{aligned} $$

So, the required Fourier transform is,

$$ f(t) \longleftrightarrow e^{-\frac{w^2}{2}}=e^{-\frac{4 \pi^2 f^2}{2}}=e^{-2 \pi^2 f^2} $$

$$ f(t) \longleftrightarrow e^{-\pi(\sqrt{2 \pi} f)^2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

From standard result, Fourier transform only Gaussian function results in Gaussian, i.e.

$$ e^{-\pi t^2} \longleftrightarrow e^{-\pi t^2} $$

To obtain a transform as given in equation (i), applying time scaling property, replacing ' $t$ ' by $\frac{t}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}$ , we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & e^{-\pi\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}\right)^2} \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 / \sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{-\pi\left(\frac{f}{1 / \sqrt{2 \pi}}\right)^2} \\ & e^{-\frac{t^2}{2}} \longleftrightarrow \sqrt{2 \pi} e^{-2 \pi^2 f^2} \\ & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{-\frac{t^2}{2}} \longleftrightarrow e^{-2 \pi^2 f^2} \end{aligned} $$

Comparing with equation (i),

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & f(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{-\frac{t^2}{2}} \\ \therefore & f(0)=\left.\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} e^{\frac{-t^2}{2}}\right|_{t=0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \\\therefore & f(0)<1 \end{array} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content:
Consider a continuous time signal $x(t)$ defined by $x(t)=0$ for $|t|>1$ and $x(t)=1-|t|$ for $|t| \leq 1$
Let the Fourier transform of $x(t)$ be defined as $X(\omega)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j \omega t} d t$. The maximum magnitude of $X(\omega)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
Given : $x(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}0, & |t|>1 \\ 1-|t|, & |t| \leq 1\end{array}\right.$

Comparing $x(t)$ with standard definition of triangular function,

$$ \operatorname{Atri}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 0, & |t|>\tau \\ A\left[1-\frac{|t|}{\tau}\right], & |t| \leq \tau \end{array}\right. $$

So, given $x(t)$ is a triangular function of amplitude $A=1$ and half width $\tau=1$ as shown in figure,

$$ x(t)=\operatorname{tri}(t) $$

From standard result,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & A \operatorname{tri}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) \longleftrightarrow A \tau \operatorname{sinc}^2(f \tau) \text { or } A \tau S a^2\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2}\right) \\ & \operatorname{tri}(t) \stackrel{A=1}{\tau=1} \operatorname{sinc}^2(f) \text { or } S a^2\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \\ \therefore & X(\omega)=S a^2\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)=\left\{S a\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\right\}^2 \\ & S a\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)} \end{array} $$

Peak value of $S a\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ is 1 which occurs at $\omega=0$ as,

$$ \lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin t}{t}=1 $$

As, $$ X(\omega)=\left[S a\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\right]^2 $$

Hence, maximum magnitude of $X(\omega)$ is,

$$ |X(\omega)|_{\max }=(1)^2=1 $$

Hence, the maximum magnitude of $X(\omega)$ is 1 .

content: Suppose x1(t) and x2(t) have the Fourier transforms as shown below. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: x<sub>1</sub>(t) and x<sub>2</sub>(t) are complex and x<sub>1</sub>(t)x<sub>2</sub>(t) is also complex with nonzero imaginary part
x<sub>1</sub>(t) and x<sub>2</sub>(t)  are real and x<sub>1</sub>(t)x<sub>2</sub>(t) is also real
x<sub>1</sub>(t) and x<sub>2</sub>(t)  are complex but x<sub>1</sub>(t)x<sub>2</sub>(t) is real
x<sub>1</sub>(t) and x<sub>2</sub>(t)  are imaginary but x<sub>1</sub>(t)x<sub>2</sub>(t) is real

Consider a signal defined by $$$x\left(t\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}e^{j10t}\;\;\;for\;\left|t\right|\leq1\\0\;\;\;\;\;\;\;for\;\;\left|t\right|>1\end{array}\right.$$$
Its Fourier Transform is

Let f(t) be a continuous time signal and let F($$\omega$$) be its Fourier Transform defined by $$F\left(\omega\right)=\int_{-\infty}^\infty f\left(t\right)e^{-j\omega t}dt$$. Define g(t) by $$g\left(t\right)=\int_{-\infty}^\infty F\left(u\right)e^{-jut}du$$. What is the relationship between f(t) and g(t)?

options.content: g(t) would always be proportional to f(t)
g(t) would be proportional to f(t) if f(t) is an even function
g(t) would be proportional to f(t) only if f(t) is a sinusoidal function
g(t) would never be proportional to f(t)

content:
A differentiable non constant even function x(t) has a derivative y(t), and their respective
Fourier Transforms are X($$\omega$$) and Y($$\omega$$). Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: X($$\omega$$) and Y($$\omega$$) are both real.
X($$\omega$$) is real and Y($$\omega$$) is imaginary.
X($$\omega$$) and Y($$\omega$$) are both imaginary.
X($$\omega$$) is imaginary and Y($$\omega$$) is real.

content:
A 10 kHz even-symmetric square wave is passed through a bandpass filter with centre
frequency at 30 kHz and 3 dB passband of 6 kHz. The filter output is

options.content: a highly attenuated square wave at 10 kHz
a nearly perfect cosine wave at 30 kHz.
a nearly perfect sine wave at 30 kHz.
